elements.sent-2411-001: Cartesian tree matching is a form of generalized pattern matching where a substring of the text matches with the pattern if they share the same Cartesian tree.
elements.sent-2411-002: This form of matching finds application for time series of stock prices and can be of interest for melody matching between musical scores.
elements.sent-2411-003: For the indexing problem, the state-of-the-art data structure is a Burrows--Wheeler transform based solution due to [Kim and Cho, CPM'21], which uses nearly succinct space and can count the number of substrings that Cartesian tree match with a pattern in time linear in the pattern length.
elements.sent-2411-004: The authors address the construction of their data structure with a straight-forward solution that, however, requires pointer-based data structures, which asymptotically need more space than compact solutions [Kim and Cho, CPM'21, Section~A.4].
elements.sent-2411-005: We address this bottleneck by a construction that requires compact space and has a time complexity linear in the product of the text length with some logarithmic terms.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, we can extend this index for indexing multiple circular texts in the spirit of the extended Burrows--Wheeler transform without sacrificing the time and space complexities.
elements.sent-2411-007: We present this index in a dynamic variant, where we pay a logarithmic slowdown and need compact space for the extra functionality that we can incrementally add texts.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our extended setting is of interest for finding repetitive motifs common in the aforementioned applications, independent of offsets and scaling.
elements.sent-2411-009: String matching is ubiquitous in computer science, and its variations are custom-made to solve a wide variety of problems.
elements.sent-2411-010: We here focus on a special kind of variation called substring consistent equivalence relation (SCER)~\cite{matsuoka16generalized .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Two strings $X$ and $Y$ are said to SCER-match if they have the same length and all substrings of equal length starting at the same text position SCER-match, i.e., $X[i..j]$ SCER-matches with $Y[i..j]$ for all $1 \le i \le j \le |X| = |Y|$.
elements.sent-2411-012: A specific instance of SCER-matching is order-preserving matching~\cite{kubica13orderpreserving,kim14orderpreserving , which has been studied for the analysis of numerical time series.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The aim of order-preserving matching is to match two strings if the relative order of the symbols in both strings is the same.
elements.sent-2411-014: Order-preserving matching therefore can find matches independently of value offsets and scaling.
elements.sent-2411-015: Since order-preserving matching takes the global order of the symbols in a string into account, it may be too strict in applications that primarily consider the local ordering of ranges partitioned by the peaks.
elements.sent-2411-016: In fact, time series of stock prices are such a case, where a common pattern called the head-and-shoulder~\cite{fu07stock involves one absolute peak (head) and neighboring local peaks (shoulders), where each of these neighboring peaks can be treated individually to match similar head-and-shoulder patterns with slightly changed local peaks.}
elements.sent-2411-017: For such a reason, Park et al.~\cite{park20patternsperiods proposed Cartesian tree matching.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Cartesian tree matching relaxes the notion of order-preserving matching in the sense that an order-preserving match is always a Cartesian tree match, but not necessarily the other way around.
elements.sent-2411-019: For instance, the two strings of digits $1647253$ and $2537164$ fit into the head-and-shoulder pattern, do not order-preserving match, but Cartesian tree match.
elements.sent-2411-020: For that, Cartesian tree matching compares the Cartesian trees of two strings to decide whether they match.
elements.sent-2411-021: The Cartesian tree~\cite{vuillemin80unifying is a binary tree built upon an array of numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-022: If all numbers are distinct, it is the min-heap whose in-order traversal retrieves the original array.
elements.sent-2411-023: Since its inception, Cartesian tree matching and variations thereof have attracted interest from researchers, whose studies include multiple pattern matching~\cite{park20patternsperiods,song21fastmatchingalg , approximate matching~\cite{auvray23approximateswaps,kim24approximate , substring matching~\cite{faro22ctmsubstring , subsequence matching~\cite{oizumi22subsequence , indeterminate matching~\cite{gawrychowski20indeterminate , cover matching~\cite{kikuchi20covers , and palindromic matching~\cite{funakoshi24computing .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In addition to stock prices, applications of generalized pattern matching can also be found in melody matching between musical scores.
elements.sent-2411-025: Pattern matching music scores such as differences, directions, or magnitudes have been studied~\cite{hiraga97structural .}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, the detection of repetitions in a piece of music has also been considered of importance~\cite{foster12method .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The question therefore is whether we can find repetitive substrings that Cartesian tree match with a repetition of a set of melodic motifs (i.e., input texts).
elements.sent-2411-028: For that to be efficient, we want to index these motifs.
elements.sent-2411-029: An index for Cartesian tree matching of a text string $T$ is a data structure built upon $T$ that can report the number of substrings of $T$ that Cartesian tree match a given pattern.
elements.sent-2411-030: Given $T$ has $n$ symbols drawn from an integer alphabet of size~$\sigma$, Park et al.~\cite{park20patternsperiods and Nishimoto et al.~\cite{nishimoto21heaps proposed indexes for Cartesian tree matching of $T$ that both occupy $O(n \lg n)$ bits of space and are constructed with $O(n \lg n)$ and $O(n \sigma \lg n)$ additional bits of space, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Park et al.~\cite[Section~5.1]{park20patternsperiods achieve $O(m \lg \sigma)$ time and Nishimoto et al.~\cite[Section~5.1]{nishimoto21heaps $O(m (\sigma + \lg m) + occ)$ time for answering count queries, where $occ$ is the number of occurrences of the pattern of length~$m$.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Adapting Ferragina and Mantaci's FM-index for exact string matching~\cite{ferragina00fmindex , Kim and Cho~\cite{kim21compact proposed an index that occupies $3n + o(n)$ bits of space, and answers a count query in $O(m)$ time for a pattern of length~$m$.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: For construction, they proposed a straight-forward solution that takes as input the Cartesian suffix tree of Park et al.~\cite{park20patternsperiods , which, however, requires $O(n \lg n)$ additional bits of space.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We address two goals in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-035: The first is a construction algorithm for Kim and Cho's index that takes compact working space.
elements.sent-2411-036: Second, while all aforementioned indexes can partially address the problem by indexing multiple texts, it is hard to detect whether the pattern is a repetition of one of the input texts, which is of interest in case of indexing melodic motifs that can repeat.
elements.sent-2411-037: Here, our goal is an index that can find such matches, even if they start with different offsets of the same repetition.
elements.sent-2411-038: In concrete words, our aim is to index multiple texts for Cartesian pattern matching.
elements.sent-2411-039: The search space for a pattern are the texts that are considered to be infinite concatenations with themselves.
elements.sent-2411-040: In this paper, we propose an extension of the index of Kim and Cho~\cite{kim21compact for Cartesian tree matching with techniques of the extended Burrows--Wheeler transform~\cite{mantaci07ebwt , and call the resulting data structure the cBWT index.}}
